World War I was a pivotal moment in the twentieth century in terms of reinventing societal roles. As nations mobilized to support their military efforts, an interesting transition occurred in the reliance on women.  Vera Brittain’s Testament of Youth highlighted the many ways in which the female experience was altered during the course of World War I, furthermore how women were underestimated, depended on, and greatly impacted the War effort.Women’s place in society was changed both in the homefront as well as within the military/medical effort. Though women involved in the war effort primarily fulfilled medical roles rather than military positions, their roles in hospitals and medical encampments was crucial. Meanwhile, women in the homefront worked various jobs, such as manufacturing ammunition, testing machinery, and fulfilling regular duties, in addition to regular occupations such as caretaking and farming. Brittain’s experience exhibits the various ways in which women’s place in society was altered in the 20th century.
Brittain’s dedication to aiding the war effort is eminent throughout her retelling of her experience as a nurse. Brittain recalls her childhood in Buxton, and her perceived future as a scholar at Oxford. The Great War redirected her plans, and as the war progressed, Brittain enlisted into medical services. Towards the beginning of the novel, Brittain recounts how many people saw education as a fitting time for women to find husbands. Brittain disagreed with this, and noted the sexist dialogue that surrounded her. She recalled the beginning of her nursing career, and the continuous obstacles women faced, writing “the shortage of doctors…must be a tremendous problem–yet when the women doctors they are crying out for now began their training, every possible obstacle was put in their way,” (Brittain 109). Brittain’s narration of the obstacles put in front of women, as well as her resilience to overcome them, demonstrates the tenacity of women of the World War I era.

Karel Capek’s 1936 novel War with the Newts is a captivating science fiction story which examines European politics and worldwide social commentary. Karel Capek, a Czech author born in 1890, also wrote Rossum’s Universal Robots in 1920, a play that explores sentient robots and their effect on society. Overall, his works are mostly science fiction that echo many political themes of his era, some dealing with subjects that have yet to occur. Many of the topics and ideas discussed in War with the Newts predates the Second World War, but is eerily similar to events that unfold. The plot itself follows the discovery and eventual exploitation of a Newt population, and explores their interaction with human society in various parts of the world.
The book declines to follow one specific protagonist or character, which I find amusing. Instead, the book is separated into three sections. The first section deals with the discovery of a newt-like species living near an island off the coast of Sumatra. Captain J. van Toch, a recurring character, discovers the species and quickly realizes that they can assist him in his goal of pearl farming. Overall, the tone of this section is the most satirical of the three.  Captain J. van Toch eventually approaches Mr Gussie H. Bondy, a financially powerful industrialist. The captain inquires with Mr. Bondy about the possibilities of these Newts while still maintaining a sense of autonomy for them. Following J. van Toch’s later death, Mr. Bondy declares a new era of Newt interactions is upon the human race, and that they must be used for more intense, industrialist work such as building infrastructure. Various scientists interact with the Newts and declare that their “intelligence should not be overestimated, as it in no way surpasses that of the average modern man,” which is a sentiment shared by many of the people that interact with the Newts, consistently underestimating and undervaluing them (Capek 112). The film is set in Hong Kong, beginning in 1962. At the start of the film, we are introduced to Chow Mo-wan, who moves into an apartment downtown with his spouse. The overcrowded apartment is full of unique individuals, but Chow Mo-wan is particularly struck with his diagonal neighbor, Su Li-zhen. Both Chow and Su find themselves slowly indulging in each other’s company in order to cope with the excessive absences of their spouses–both of which are revealed to be involved in adulterous activities. Though Chow and Su make an effort not to immerse themselves romantically, their relationship slowly grows more and more passionate, and the two develop a unique bond as they navigate their shared experience. 
As the film progresses, some scenes are integrated with slow-motion clips that are accompanied by a suspenseful yet playful score while painting the lives of Su and Chow. Oftentimes, Chow and Su will be walking in similar areas and barely avoid each other on the street, depicting the isolation and sadness the protagonists are living in while so close to one another. Their proximity to each other decreases with each passing, a symbol of their shared living situation. Kar-Wai’s film is also an aesthetically pleasing, visually stunning film. The color gradients, in accompaniment with well-blocked frames, creates a captivating feeling throughout the film. It is stunning to watch, the acting is passionate, and the writing is well-developed. 
Su Li-zhen faces several challenges due to her position in society as a woman. Within her profession as a secretary. Her boss expects her to be hardworking yet elegant, imposing social pressures of complacency and modesty onto Su. As her relationship with Chow progresses with Chow, she fears doing something maritally improper, despite her husband’s blatant disregard for his marital commitments. As Su and Chow further their relationship, Su even practices confronting her husband with Chow. As a result of that, she finds herself seeking greater emotional support within Chow. This tender moment portrays how she is able to garner strength through her relationship with Chow while she grapples with the guilt of her marriage. At one point, in an exchange with Chow, she asks “Am I hopeless?” in reference to her failing marriage. For women of this era, and especially in Asia, divorce was frowned upon heavily. For Su, the prospect of divorce was framed as a plaguing element of her future that would affect her social position and relationships. This inner fear and inescapable disappointment proves difficult for Su, and causes a lot of anxiety within her growing relationship with Chow. While Wong Kar-Wai analyzes the female experience through Su, he simultaneously explores the greater historical context of this era.

original_text:
The article from USA Today offers a deep and personal look into the lives of individuals in Michigan who have battled with eating disorders, sharing their unique experiences, challenges, and paths toward recovery. It presents a collection of stories that highlight not only the struggles and dangers associated with eating disorders but also the hope and possibility for healing.
The individuals' stories roughly cover the broad spectrum of symptoms associated with eating disorders, including restrictive eating, binge eating, purging, and excessive exercise (although this is something we didn’t really cover in class). These symptoms align with clinical descriptions of disorders like anorexia nervosa, bulimia nervosa, and binge-eating disorder, showcasing a wide array of behaviors and physical health consequences, from malnutrition to the psychological toll of these conditions. The article points to societal pressures, like the glorification of thinness and the influence of social media, as well as personal and psychological factors, including trauma, the need for control, and issues of self-worth. The diversity of these factors in the stories told emphasizes that eating disorders do not have a single cause but rather emerge from a complex interplay of individual, environmental, and societal influences.
In terms of treatment, the article vividly illustrates the idea that recovery from eating disorders is a multifaceted and non-linear process that includes therapy, nutritional counseling, and sometimes medical intervention. It highlights the challenges of accessing appropriate care, the shortcomings of health systems that rely on inadequate measures like BMI for diagnoses, and the importance of personalized support. The narrative strongly advocates for a holistic understanding of health that transcends physical appearance, underscoring the role of mental health professionals, dietitians, and supportive communities in the recovery process. 
The report from USA Today accurately reflects the current understanding of eating disorders in terms of symptoms, etiology, and treatment. It provides a crucial humanizing lens on the issue, moving beyond clinical descriptions to the lived experiences of individuals. This approach not only increases awareness but also challenges stereotypes and misconceptions about eating disorders. However, while the article does an excellent job of illustrating the personal and emotional dimensions of these disorders, it could be complemented by more detailed information on the specific types of treatments available and the evidence supporting them. It’s less of an article exploring the psychology and science of eating disorders, but more of an anecdotal story appealing to the emotional aspects of these disorders. Moreover, a more explicit discussion on the role of genetic and biological factors in the etiology of eating disorders could provide a more comprehensive view of these complex conditions, like we did in class. In addition to this, our lectures and textbook talked considerably less about the usage of dieticians, and explored the role of therapy more significantly.

Undergraduate roommate relationships can play a role in shaping students' academic and social experiences. Traditionally, roommates can be assigned randomly or made via existing friendships, but the prevalence of social media has turned it into a space for potential roommate seeking. It is becoming more and more common to see Facebook groups and Instagram pages with incoming freshmen students introducing themselves and seeking potential roommates and friends. The nature of these roommate relationships, whether formed online or assigned randomly, can significantly impact stress levels, personal health, and academic performance (Dusselier et al., 2005), suggesting that the method of roommate pairing might be as influential as the quality of the relationship itself. The present study explores the methods of roommate pairing, especially the effects of meeting on social media, while also taking into account the total amount of interpersonal interactions between the roommates in terms of the quantity of conflict resolution discussions.
The study by Eisenberg et al. (2014) provides some understanding of peer effects within university environments, particularly focusing on how students' behaviors are influenced by their roommates. Participants took a survey upon move-in and another near the end of the first year. The study found notable peer effects on binge drinking, while there was less evidence for peer effects on other activities such as gambling. Having a roommate who engaged in binge drinking increased the likelihood that a student would also engage in binge drinking. The magnitude of this effect was substantial, indicating that peer influence could be a strong factor in the prevalence of binge drinking among roommates. The results highlight the role of peers, especially roommates, in shaping students' health and social practices.
Some exploration on the effects of non-academic obligations was done by Erb et al. (2014). First generation college students were interviewed by researchers to explore the impacts of their familial obligations and diverse backgrounds on roommate relationships. Interviews suggested that these students often carried significant family responsibilities into their college life, which limited their engagement with roommates from different family settings. This situation frequently led to difficulties in adjusting to and connecting with their roommates, showing the challenges faced in nurturing supportive living environments at college. The interviews provided rich insights into the intersection of family dynamics and adjusting to college, highlighting the unique struggles of first-generation college students in navigating roommate relationships.
While many of these studies examine roommates’ effects on social behavior, Yakusheva et al. (2011) investigated the peer effects on weight gain among first-year college students. These students, who were required to live in on-campus dormitories and were mostly randomly assigned roommates, provided baseline data upon entering college and follow-up data at the end of the academic year. Yakusheva et al. found that students' exposure to their roommates' eating and exercise habits significantly influenced their own weight-related behaviors.

original_text:
Respondents will engage with a series of structured questionnaires designed to assess the perceived strength of friendship and the level of concordance with their roommates. After the initial survey determines roommate assignment method, the remaining eligible participants will take another survey upon move-in to determine baseline data. At the end of the semester, before winter break, the respondents will be surveyed again.
To measure the frequency of conflict resolution, we adapted a conflict resolution scale previously validated in peer relationship studies, called the Conflicts and Problem Solving Scales (Kerig, 1995). Because the CPS was originally designed by Kerig to measure four dimensions of conflict between couples (frequency, severity, resolution, and efficacy), we altered it to prioritize frequency, measured as a rating of the number of times roommates engage in both minor and major conflicts in a month, set on a 0 (never) to 7 (multiple days a week) scale. See Appendix A.
In addition, respondents will answer questions based on aspects of efficacy as well, with items such as “We can openly discuss our differences,” concerning the potential suppression of conflict resolution discussions, and “When we have a disagreement, we usually come to a mutually agreeable solution,” both measured using a 0 (strongly disagree) to 5 (strongly agree) Likert scale.
Other items, such as for severity, include “We usually act friendly to each other after an argument,” also measured using a 0 (strongly disagree) to 5 (strongly agree) Likert scale.
Respondents will also complete a survey for concordance, which is similar to the Likert scales of our conflict resolution scale, while it also contains some areas in which living together will certainly effect. These include: cleanliness and organization, sleeping and study habits, socialization preferences, the use of shared items or spaces, financial matters (like sharing costs of dorm items), and leisure and entertainment preferences.
An example of an item measuring concordance of organization is “My roommate and I have similar standards of cleanliness," with a 0 (strongly disagree) to 5 (strongly agree) Likert scale. A similar example for socialization would be "We have similar views on having friends over in our room/dorm," utilizing the same scale.
To gauge the strength of the perceived friendship, we will use a modified version of the Interpersonal Relationship Scale (IRS). This questionnaire, which is longer than the conflict resolution scale, includes items that cover Rawlins’ Interactional Dialectics theory. See Appendix B.
For example, the item "I can be sure that my roommate will help me whenever I ask for it." reflects the practical aspect of the roommate relationship, indicating reliance on the roommate for help, which is a form of instrumental support in the affection vs. instrumentality portion of the theory.

original_text: As Prime Minister since 2019, Mette Frederiksen has demonstrated transformational leadership by steering Denmark through various challenges, including the COVID-19 pandemic and geopolitical tensions. Her leadership during the pandemic involved decisive actions and clear communication, aiming to protect public health and maintain societal stability. Frederiksen has also been pivotal in enhancing Denmark's defense policies, notably by initiating diplomatic talks in early 2022 regarding the potential presence of American troops on Danish soil, reflecting a proactive approach to national and regional security. These initiatives align with key criteria of transformational leadership. Inspirational Motivation: Frederiksen articulates a clear and compelling vision for Denmark's future, especially concerning public health and security. Idealized Influence: She leads by example, making tough decisions during crises, thereby earning public trust and respect. Intellectual Stimulation: Frederiksen encourages innovative solutions to complex problems, such as exploring new defense collaborations. Her transformative efforts focus on strengthening Denmark's resilience in health and security sectors. While her policies have garnered broad support, they have also faced opposition. For instance, her decision to cull mink during the pandemic was later deemed unconstitutional, leading to public and political criticism. Additionally, discussions about hosting U.S. troops sparked debates about Denmark's sovereignty and foreign policy direction.

original_text: In short, the most dangerous part of coronavirus is the speed of transmission. Moreover, coronavirus detection is more complex than SARS because of the asymptomatic transmission period.  Every disease has an incubation period, where the virus is dormant and symptoms are not expressed. In diseases like SARS, the disease cannot be spread during this incubation period, but in coronavirus, people who don’t even know that they are affected can spread it around. Coronavirus has already affected Bay Area communities, and likely will infect areas in the Tri-Valley. Healthy individuals, such as most students and parents, have an increasingly large chance of survival. As technology advances and more resources are put into the coronavirus, the CFR progressively decreases. “The rate of how the coronavirus is spreading scares my family, especially with all the measures being put in place contributing to some of the panic,” said Leo Jenson. Wizards, What do you think of when you hear that word? Probably something along the lines of “movies”, “fake”, or even the odd “Harry potter”. Everyone knows that Magic isn’t real right? Well I’m here to tell you otherwise.
I’m Ferris Harry James Walden the 3rd, but I just go by Ferris. Now, the good news is, that if you’re reading this, that means I survived long enough to finish writing everything down, WooHoo! Or you stole it from my room, shame on you if you did. So back to where I was, yes, magic is real, and I don’t mean like pull a rabbit out of the hat, guess your card real, I mean fire and flying and all the perks that come with being a mage like me. You might be asking, “Wait hold on, I like flying and fire, why can’t I be a mage and fly?”, The honest answer? No one really knows, it’s related to genetics, but no one is sure what exactly makes people mages and others not. Sometimes there will be no magic in a family and then Boom! Magic Child. Sometimes it will be the opposite, where there’s a whole family tree of mages and then one is born without power. Me, neither of my parents were mages, or theirs, so it made my teen years really awkward. Instead of coming home from school with a detention slip for cheating on a test or getting in a fight, it was because someone lit one of the classrooms on fire, and, my personal favorite, “Your son was a disruption in the class today, he managed to flip three desks, with students in them, without getting up out of his seat. You need to discuss with him the consequences of harming other classmates with foolish pranks like that. ” Okay enough about me, back to magic. Magic is different for everyone, but it shares the same concept.
